After action(['UP','UP','LEFT']) there are three entries -- first UP, second UP, then LEFT. All change within one entry's frames is that single action animating, never several actions. Step through entries to read per-move motion and causality.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.

You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

- Plan: Try basic movement keys to see what moves

end of world model.

sections.content: Let me check what changed after pressing RIGHT. I need to compare the before and after frames to see what moved.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Compare before and after frames to see what changed
before = last_transition.before_frame.segmentation
after = last_transition.after_frame.segmentation

print("BEFORE RIGHT:")
for node in before['nodes']:
    b = node['boundary']
    rows = [p[0] for p in b]
    cols = [p[1] for p in b]
    print(f"  id={node['id']}, color={node['color']}, pixels={node['pixels']}, bbox=({min(rows)},{min(cols)})-({max(rows)},{max(cols)})")

print("\nAFTER RIGHT:")
for node in after['nodes']:
    b = node['boundary']
    rows = [p[0] for p in b]
    cols = [p[1] for p in b]
    print(f"  id={node['id']}, color={node['color']}, pixels={node['pixels']}, bbox=({min(rows)},{min(cols)})-({max(rows)},{max(cols)})")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I can see what changed:
1. The green bar (N) at top shrank from 64 pixels to 62 pixels, and the last 2 pixels became white (W) - this looks like a timer/progress bar that's counting down
2. The blue bar (b) moved from cols 12-31 to cols 16-35 - it shifted RIGHT by 4 columns!
3. All other objects stayed the same position
So RIGHT moves the blue bar to the right. The green bar at top seems to be a timer/steps counter that decreases.
Let me try more movements to understand the full mechanics. The blue bar seems to be the player-controlled object. Let me try to see if I can move it to interact with other objects.
The blue bar is at rows 16-19, cols 16-35 (after RIGHT). The yellow blocks are at rows 52-59. The charcoal+magenta stack is at rows 1-7, cols 36-39.
Let me try DOWN to see if the blue bar moves down, and also try to understand the goal.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: World model updated: RIGHT moves the blue bar 4 columns right. The green bar at top is a timer/steps counter (shrinking). The blue bar is the controllable object.

</parameter>
